speaker: interviewer
text: --is Zora Goldberger. I want to go back and ask you very quickly, what year did you marry your second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What year? In-- in '45. In '45 in Bari, Italy. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in '49 in United State through those people what I was selling last time who sent us the paper. And I remember, but first I remember we came with the ship, That was I think 13 days was on ship. It was wonderful. And I remember when we arrived to New York, when we saw Statue of Liberty, I-- all of a sudden, I started to cry. I says, oh, my god, completely new life. And I cannot describe what I felt, happiness that finally we going to be OK. I had a feeling. Now, we're going to be OK. But it was a frightened feeling, because you came to State. I didn't know English at all. My husband did, but I didn't. And we-- we-- we were on the ship yet. And all of a sudden, our name was called. Julius Goldberger, your uncle is here. We didn't even know about uncle. And this uncle came with a Cadillac. And he took us to New York to a little-- to cafeteria. And he says, children, now eat something. And he gave my husband $5. Here is $5. And go to Chicago. And my husband says, no. I don't want to go right away. I want to rest, because my wife is not so well. And we need couple days' rest. He says, OK. But here is Jewish organization. And he took us there. And we stayed three days in New York. At least, I saw a little bit what it's you have to see, the Radio City and Statue of Liberty. We stay three days. And then we went with a train to Chicago to our sponsors, Alfred and Francis Goldberger. When we came to station here, Union Station, they were waiting for us. I remember we hugged each other. I started to cry. And the only thing I could say, I love you. I love you. I love you. That's all I could say. And they took us in their home. They were living on Argyle. I don't remember the number, but on Argyle. They had a two-bedrooms apartment. I remember that. And they took a room. They put us in. And she opened the closet with the clothes. She says, Zora, that's all yours. And we stayed with them a couple weeks. And then we had to go. One day, she came crying. She says, you know, I cannot take you anymore. Because my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no. She was living in apartment. And they says that they have to move if we stayed there with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they find us a little room. We were there I don't know how long. And we met some people who were born in Podravska Slatina, older couple. Steiner, their name is Steiner. And we called them Daddy and Ma. They are just wonderful to-- to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband-- no, before that, somebody told me that my girlfriend is here in Chicago who I knew from Slatina. She was refugee from Vienna. And they were all staying, 200 people, they stayed in a hotel when I was a young girl yet. I wasn't married yet. They all stayed there. And she was a beautiful girl. I remember her. And she married a guy from Slatina. That's how she is safe, because her family was disappeared lately. And they came to Chicago. So I heard about that. And I didn't know her address. I only knew that she's on Argyle somebody told me. So we went from house to house until I find his name, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ring the bell. And I remember his mother opened the door. And he opened the door. And she looked. And then this [? Josephine, ?] her name is [? Josephine, ?] my girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came. Oh, my god, Zora. Zora. She starts to scream, because she didn't know that I am living. And she called right away other friend from Slatina who was there, who lives in Chicago, a very well-to-do man. And they all came. And they were so nice to us. They invited us for dinners. And really they were wonderful. But that's not what I wanted to say. I wanted to say something else. Oh, yeah. Then this guy, he had a cleaning-- not cleaning store, but cleaning plant. So he wanted to give my husband job. Because-- oh, the first job when we came has to be sponsored. And he was like laborer. So he went one day. And when they saw him, he says, sorry, we cannot take you. There's no job for you. They just didn't want to take him. He says you are-- after all, my husband was a lawyer. And he was dressed and good-looking. He didn't look like a poor immigrant. They didn't want to keep him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then a friend of mine says, you know what? Why don't you come to work in my plant. And he did for about-- I don't know how many weeks. Then he got sick. He couldn't do it. He didn't know much about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we find some people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This Daddy, the Steiner who I told you, this old couple, they find a job for my husband in insurance company. And he started that. And he was there all his year. He changed the company, but he was there. But Jewish organization find us a little place to live. They gave us some furniture, bedroom, and for kitchen set. I was very happy, because that was mine, first time. And they wanted to give me a job and that my husband should the study the law. I mean, not study, because he has to have a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to say it. I know how to say it. But right now, I am too excited. I don't know how to. Probably he is going to tell you. That he should do study and I should work. And my husband said, oh, no. My wife won't work. She never worked. I-- I got a job And I'm going to study in the night. And in beginning, he did. But he couldn't. He couldn't have a job, because this job was very demanding. And so I-- I didn't work for a while. Then I started to speak. I never went to school. But I picked up, because I wanted. I went all by myself in buses, because I wanted to be independent. And then I wanted to have a job, too. I remember I got Sinclair company. And I went for interview. And they asked me experience. I says, sorry, but I don't have experience. He says, that's OK. I take you anyway. And I was working there three months [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] filing. And after three months, I don't know what happened. I had to quit. And I didn't work anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then one day, I got pregnant. And that was the happiest day of-- but before that, I had a surgery. I had ovary removed, one. And I thought, oh, I never will have children. And one day, after seven years, I got pregnant. And I had my daughter. And that's the happiest day of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in '56. 1956 she was born. And she was a girl. And I wanted so badly a girl. And she was girl. I was so happy. And I remember my husband told me, oh, she's so ugly. Because they show her right away to him. And they didn't clean her. He came to me crying. He says, she's so ugly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they brought me, she was-- I says, how can you tell ugly? She's so beautiful. And she was beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I didn't work. I-- I was all the time with my baby, because I was so afraid not to lose her. And when she started to school, she wanted to be a little bit more independent. She says, mommy, you baby me too much. The older girls, the-- the mothers work. They were all, you know, independent. And she wanted that, too. But somehow I always cling to her. Then when she-- when she finished the school, before she started high school, then I says, OK. Maybe I should start to work. But I didn't, because my husband got very ill. Terry was five years old then. He was taken to the hospital. And after couple days, the doctor called me. And he says, you know, your husband has leukemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, gosh. I thought I'm going to die. I says, what-- what's going to happen now? He says, if he is going to survive five years, then it's OK. But five years are critical in his case.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was desperate. Nobody could tell him. He didn't know that he has-- he thought he had some kind of virus. Because he had to go always for checkup. And they took blood and born marrow tests. But he never have suspected that he has leukemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought, what's going to happen now to me and the baby? If something happened, I'm not prepared to be all alone again. But somehow, he was OK. He worked. He went to hospital. He worked. And it was still-- she finished the school. And I says, maybe now it's time that I started to work to have a little bit independent. And, again, what to do? I didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barnaby Taylor started to open the new store. And I went for interview. I says, I would like to have a job. Experience, none.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, you know what? You don't need experience. I like your appearance. I like your accent. You're going to do a wonderful job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me. And I was there for 15 years. That was very happy for me. I had a wonderful job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Terry graduated high school. Then she started college. And she finished the college. And then she met this guy. And she got married in-- in-- '70-- when did she get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 21 years. Anyway, she got married. And all of a sudden, I felt so good. My worries were over. Because I thought if anything happened, at least she is going to be provided. And I started to be more independent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Yeah. OK. Don't want to talk about that. Then after 12 years of marriage, Terry one day called me. She says, mother, [? Eric ?] wants to leave me. He doesn't want to get married. I took the first plane to New York. And I talked to him. He says, I love Terry. But I don't want to be married. That's all. And they had a beautiful home there. They lived in Commack in Long Island. So, OK. Terry, they sold the house. And she took a little studio apartment. I was with her for about week or two. Then I came back. And in meantime, one day in the night I got terrible chest pain. And my husband took me in hospital. I had a little heart attack, very light heart attack. I was in hospital, I don't know, for a week. Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I was emotional-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emotionally not OK. So I started to go to Yugoslavia for treatment. Because I developed asthma and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So on Adriatic Sea, Hvar-- the name is Hvar, H-V-A-R-- it's a place for asthma. They treat them. So I went there for six years every year for three months. What was that like the first time to go back to Yugoslavia after all that had happened to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After all-- you see, first time we went in '72 all of three. Because we wanted to show Terry where we were born. She was 13 years old then. And oh, she fell in love. Because I find out that I have two cousins still living, one in Zagreb, one is Sarajevo. And my husband got a cousin there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to see them. But we stayed only eight days in Yugoslavia. We stayed a couple days with them, and then we went to Dubrovnik. But Terry just fell in love with-- my daughter, with Yugoslavia. So for three years, every year she went for vacation there. And she started-- she never learned how to speak Yugoslavian. Because we talked to her always in English. And she resented when we talked. She says, mother, you are American. I don't want you to hear anymore talking Croatian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did I want to say with that? You were in treatment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, yeah, after my heart attack, when I started to go to Yugoslavia, one day I come back. And I started to feel sick. I didn't feel too good. I hardly could walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me to the doctor, to a cardiologist. And he took some angiogram. And he says, I need angioplasty. So I had that done. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was OK. I-- I still have to watch myself, you know. I'm on the pills. But I'm OK till this year on Christmas Day. Day before Christmas, we were invited for Christmas dinner. And always then I have terrible pain in my stomach, all day long, day before. And I didn't want to say anything, because we were invited for dinner. I didn't want to spoil. But I had such a pain that I couldn't stand anymore. So they took me in emergency in hospital. And when I was there, they took blood test. And they said, oh. They called my doctor. He came, who happens to be our friend, this Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. He says, Zora, you cannot go out. You have to stay in hospital. And we were invited by him for dinner. I says, but I can't. I have to-- No, you can't. He says, I have to tell you. I says, what's wrong. He says, they find something in your blood. And you have to stay for, you know-- And the next day, [INAUDIBLE] just came, what I didn't know who she is. And she took bone marrow. And they find out that I have leukemia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For three days, I-- they could-- I was so upset, afraid what's going to happen. But they assure me that's a very mild case. And I'll never be well, but I can live normally for many years. But I have to be all the time on this pills what I'm taking. But my blood was, you know, normally is around 4,000 to 6,000 these white cells. And I had over 260. I was really in very bad shape. And now, I am on pills. I am OK. But it goes up and down, up and down. I have-- I had to go all the time for my tests. Sometimes go nicely done. Then, again, goes up. They still cannot completely adjust. And I am-- most of the time, I am very tired. But, otherwise, I'm normal. I do everything. I don't let myself go. I always thinking about my daughter. She needs me. And I try to do my best. But it was scary experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I was afraid, what's going to happen to my daughter? She doesn't have any relatives here. What is going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she find this guy, which she married. And he's a very nice guy. He's a graphic designer. He has mother who I adore. We got along very nicely. And she is crazy about my daughter, what I'm very happy. So all of a sudden now, I'm with peace with myself. Whatever happen, I'm in peace with myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. We want to thank you so very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you so very much for letting us come into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for letting us conduct this interview. Do you have any last final words that you want to say before we end the video?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am grateful, I am thankful that I am here in United States. I am really very thankful. And I am happy, because I have my daughter, who is happy now. And I have a normal life. I'm happy, OK. I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my grandparents, my grandfather, my grandmother. My mother was in the middle on the-- in the row. On my-- on my right is her sister. Her name was Auntie Vera. On the left is Auntie Yelena. And down is Marishka, who was living-- when she got married, she was in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was this taken? In-- in Bjelovar. That's where my grandparents were living. It's a nice little city in Bjelovar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Before the first-- I don't know. My mother was-- how old was she here? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she was born-- I really don't know what year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me who this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother when she was a young girl, engaged probably. She was married when she was only 16 years old. And I got that when I went to visit in Yugoslavia. Some friends gave me. Because I-- fortunately, I don't have any picture of my family. That's the only thing I have of my mother. [INAUDIBLE] That's the picture of my younger sister. She was here 16 years old, just before they took her in a concentration camp. And that was in '41. She was only 16 years old. She beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her name is Vera. She was so pretty and so talented. She was just beautiful. This picture is from my youth, when I was a young girl with my two best girlfriend. On the right, her name is Beba, B-E-B-A. She was Serbian. And in the middle, her name is Justa, J-U-S-T-A. She was Croatian. My two best girlfriend. And on left, that's me. And we were inseparable, all the time together. I just loved them. The one on the right, unfortunately, two years ago died. But the other one is still in Zagreb, and I'm in touch with her. This is my wedding picture. We took-- my husband didn't even have a suit. He had a uniform. And whatever I have on my friend gave it to me just to take the picture. That was in Italy when we got married in '45. That's the picture what was taken 40 years after the war. When I visited Yugoslavia, I was invited, because the town made a reunion for the-- for the survivor. And on left, it's me. And next to me, it was a guy, he was Serbian, and then my girlfriend and all the people survived, not too many. This is a picture of Frances Goldberger, who brought us here in Chicago. On the left, that's Frances. And to the right, that's her daughter [? Carol. ?] And those are the people who are just like my relatives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the lady who I never will forget, because she is wonderful. And I love her. Oh. This is my daughter and her new husband. They married in August 6. And that's my pride and joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her new husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's-- my son-in-law's name is [? Mitch, ?] [? Mitch ?] Clark. And my daughter's name is Elizabeth Terry. And they are lovely. I love them! That's my family. It was taken a couple years ago, my daughter and my husband and me. And it was a happy, happy occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I want to introduce you my husband for 50 years. We just celebrated in February 28 our 50 anniversary with our daughter. And what should I say? We have ups and down, but we are happy now. Aren't we? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's all I have to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met-- as I said, that is my wife, Zora Goldberger. And we met in Italy, in Porto Re, actually, on the Adriatic Sea.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then we went through [INAUDIBLE], through a few camps in Italy. And we then went to-- we to the island of [INAUDIBLE], and then with the invasion [INAUDIBLE] ships to Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to thank you guys again for letting us come into your home and appearing on tape and letting us hear your story. Thank you very much.
